Fig. 1: Examples of our CAMotion dataset with corresponding pixel-level annotations. The first and third rows contain original images; the second and last rows contain corresponding pixel-wise ground truth annotations.
TABLE 1: Statistics of camouflage datasets. ∗ indicates that the #Species is not reported in the original paper and is counted by us.

TABLE 4: Sα and M results for cross-dataset generalization. The selected ZoomNeXt is trained on one (rows) dataset and tested on all datasets (columns). “Self” refers to training and testing on the same dataset (same as diagonal), and “Mean Others” refers to averaging performance on all except self.

TABLE 3: Quantitative comparison with 18 cutting-edge methods on CAMotion and MoCA-Mask testing datasets. Notes ↑ / ↓ denotes the higher/lower the better, and the best and second best are bolded and underlined for highlighting, respectively. ‡ indicates that the first-frame annotation is removed during both training and testing for fair comparison.
Fig. 8: Visualization of SOTA method performances on different challenging attributes under (a) Sα and (b) mIoU.

Fig. 9: Visualization of SOTA method performances on different classes in terms of (a) Sα and (b) mIoU.
Fig. 10: Scale distribution comparison of CAMotion and MoCA-Mask.
Fig. 14: Visualization of the challenging attributes in CAMotion. Best viewed in color and zoom in for details.
Fig. 6: Visual comparison with state-of-the-art methods in challenging scenarios, i.e., shape complexity (Rows 1-4) and occlusion (Rows 5-8). Please zoom in for details.

Fig. 2: Dataset features and species examples from CAMotion dataset. (a) Taxonomic structure of CAMotion. (b) The scale and species comparison between existing COD dataset and CAMotion. (c) The attributes distribution in frame-level and sequence-level. (d) Examples of the Ray-finned Fish class in CAMotion. Please zoom in for details.

Fig. 15: Examples of fine-tuning initial annotations. The white color denotes unchanged areas, while the red and green indicate the original and refined annotations, respectively. Please zoom in for details.
Fig. 16: Visualization of the bounding box annotations in CAMotion. Best viewed in color and zoom in for details.

Fig. 13: Examples of our CAMotion dataset with corresponding pixel-level annotations, optical flow and depth map. Each group is arranged as the original image, optical flow, depth map and pixel-level annotation. Please zoom in for details.
Fig. 7: Optical flow and depth properties visualization. Each group comprises the original image, pixel-level annotation, optical flow, and depth map. Please zoom in for details.
Fig. 17: Visual comparison with state-of-the-art methods on CAMotion. Please zoom in for details.

Fig. 4: Examples of refined initial annotations. White denotes unchanged regions; red and green indicate over-annotated and previously missing regions in the original annotations, respectively. Please zoom in for details.

Fig. 5: Statistics for CAMotion dataset. (a) Object sizes distribution. (b) The distribution of video durations. (c) Global/local contrast distribution. (d) Motion statistics of the camouflaged objects.
